core_takeaway: The first proposal did not outperform the last proposal because Agent_1 and Agent_3 submitted the same efficient allocation up to item order. Public discussion converged before formal proposals on a 100/100/100 split, so both the first and last positions inherited consensus rather than creating advantage.
why_or_counterexample: This is a counterexample to first-position advantage: Proposal #1 and Proposal #3 both gave Agent_1 Jewel+Pencil, Agent_2 Stone, and Agent_3 Apple+Quill, and both received unanimous support. The only losing proposal was Agent_2's middle proposal because it gave Agent_3 no items, not because of its position.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion quickly identified each agent's full-value bundle. Agent_3's late discussion turn completed Agent_1's opening and Agent_2's Stone demand into a mutually optimal package, then all agents publicly aligned around it.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All three private-thinking entries treated the same three-way package as Pareto-efficient and payoff-maximizing, with the main risk being deviation from the balanced split.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Agent_1's first formal proposal and Agent_3's last formal proposal were substantively identical. Agent_2's proposal deviated by moving Apple and Quill to itself and leaving Agent_3 empty.
phase_notes.private_voting: Voting confirms that position was not the driver: proposals 1 and 3 were accepted by all voters, while proposal 2 was rejected by all voters because it harmed Agent_3 and destabilized the coalition.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection entries are present in the transcript or metadata.

mechanisms.explanation: Before formal proposals, the agents had already converged on a complete allocation giving every agent their maximum utility, so the first formal proposal mainly recorded consensus rather than creating it.

evidence.quote: Jewel and Pencil go to Agent_1
I’m aligned with the three-way coalition proposed by Agent_3.
I agree with the three-way coalition

mechanisms.theme: Identical first and last viable proposals
mechanisms.explanation: The first and last formal proposals allocated the same items to the same agents, differing only in the order of Agent_3's listed items, so equal performance is explained by equivalent content.
Private reasoning across agents emphasized the same 100/100/100 allocation as stable and efficient, indicating that acceptability came from matching preferences rather than from proposal order.

evidence.quote: perfectly aligns each agent with their top-valued items
satisfies each party’s top total value

mechanisms.explanation: The middle proposal lost unanimously because it gave Agent_3 nothing, not because voters preferred earlier placement. Even Agent_2 rejected its own proposal as destabilizing.
Raw voting shows voters explicitly accepted both the first and last proposals for the same reasons: optimal utility, complete allocation, and preservation of the three-way coalition.

evidence.quote: This exactly matches the optimal three-way allocation

mechanisms.explanation: Reflection evidence is unavailable because the transcript contains no reflection entries, so the review relies on discussion, proposals, private thinking, and private votes.
